Update 1:
We have now transposed all of Andrew's suggestions to the correct working document, and are reviewing them individually (Giovanni and I). If we have concerns, we will leave a comment. Otherwise during the meeting today, we will consider them to be approved under consensus.

Here are the meeting notes for today:
https://cryptpad.fr/code/#/2/code/edit/dzEaOymNJ9LicrW9AoSVQz9s/

And here is the meeting link:
https://meet.goto.com/etsicyberqsc

Proposal for disabling of automatic updates. I've rewritten the requirement as well as the assessment:

```
5.5.1 REQ-SU-1  The product shall provide an easy-to-use option for the user to disable or defer automatic updates.

Note: For UC-INST this user can be the enterprise administrator  Applicability: Browser does not rely on OS for secure update  # Assessment ASS-SU-1

Reference: 

Assessment of [REQ-SU-1] 
Objective

Assess that users have an easy-to-use option for disabling or deferring automatic updates of the browser.

Preparation

- Browser in a state capable of being updated (eg build/configuration pointing at test update server, or an old build in need of update)
- Test process for triggering automatic browser update
- Locate the relevant configuration interface based on technical documentation for the product

Activities

1. Navigate to interface element
2. Disable automatic updates
3. Trigger automatic update process
4. Evaluate whether an automatic update occurs
5. Navigate to interface element and enable automatic updates
6. Trigger automatic update process
7. Evaluate whether an automatic update occurs

Evaluation

Pass:
    - Configuration element is in intuitive location (eg on a main configuration screen, or part of update process UI)
    - Browser does not perform an automatic update when disabled
    - Browser performs automatic update when enabled
Fail: Any of the above are not fulfilled.
```
content.formatted_body:
Proposal for disabling of automatic updates. I've rewritten the requirement as well as the assessment:

5.5.1 REQ-SU-1  The product shall provide an easy-to-use option for the user to disable or defer automatic updates.

Note: For UC-INST this user can be the enterprise administrator  Applicability: Browser does not rely on OS for secure update  # Assessment ASS-SU-1

Reference: 

Assessment of [REQ-SU-1] 
Objective

Assess that users have an easy-to-use option for disabling or deferring automatic updates of the browser.

Preparation

- Browser in a state capable of being updated (eg build/configuration pointing at test update server, or an old build in need of update)
- Test process for triggering automatic browser update
- Locate the relevant configuration interface based on technical documentation for the product

Activities

1. Navigate to interface element
2. Disable automatic updates
3. Trigger automatic update process
4. Evaluate whether an automatic update occurs
5. Navigate to interface element and enable automatic updates
6. Trigger automatic update process
7. Evaluate whether an automatic update occurs

Evaluation

Pass:
    - Configuration element is in intuitive location (eg on a main configuration screen, or part of update process UI)
    - Browser does not perform an automatic update when disabled
    - Browser performs automatic update when enabled
Fail: Any of the above are not fulfilled.

content.body:
Thanks Sam.  Do you think we could get away with dropping "easy-to-use" (even though I know it's in the CRA text) and the first pass bullet?

I'm rather worried that we're forcing browsers to implement a large foot gun they'll be handing users.

I'm happy to soften the term, but have tried to specify it generously in the assessment so I think some adjective is required in the requirement. Are you okay with how it appears in the assessment?

I was trying to specify it as something which wouldn't suffice for "turn on developer mode and add a custom attribute and value in the config that was found in a developer mailing list", but also preserved as much manufacturer choice as possible.
How about "Configuration element is present in the product's UI." which would preclude using command line flags, setting plist or registry entires etc.

That's still going to end up with something which ends up buried in three layers of config dialogs (which is probably good for security), but undermines the user choice which the requirement is intended for.
I think in the case of this requirement the law is detrimental to user security and the right thing for us to do is allow the letter of the law to be followed as narrowing as possible.

My issue is that either this is a requirement from the CRA that we should be upholding according to the law, or it's a bad idea and we shouldn't do it. I'm almost less comfortable adding the configuration option and then hiding it, as it creates tech-debt without achieving the goals specified by the EC.

I do think it's a bad idea and we shouldn't do it :-) but do we have that option?

Given the fact an out of date browser will very quickly be exposed to n-day attacks, it sort of undermines the rest of the requirements, as a compromised browser can no longer enforce any of the other required security boundaries.
Could we leave it out and see what the HASC assessment says?
Sadly the legislation was written before the vulnpocalypse and graphs like this: https://zerodayclock.com/
(BBIAB, stepping into a meeting)

Can we pick it up tomorrow? I get that this is a bad idea and could pose structural issues to security, but I also feel obliged to push for it, as it promotes user control/freedom and is explicit in the law. If you've got any other details like the zerodayclock then the context is helpful.
